name: Honor Global Privacy Control signal
summary: When a page ignores the browser's Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal, Lens surfaces the violation and offers the user a one-click action to file a complaint with the relevant Attorney General or Data Protection Authority.

evidence.claim: California AG has designated GPC as an opt-out signal that businesses MUST honor under CPRA §1798.135(b)(1); website non-compliance is a CPRA violation. CA AG has issued public enforcement notices confirming this.
Colorado AG recognized GPC as a Universal Opt-Out Mechanism effective July 1, 2024 under 4 CCR 904-3.
GPC technical specification and browser support documentation (W3C Community Group).

body.description: When the user's browser is sending GPC=1 but the target site behaves as if it has opt-in consent for sale/sharing of personal data (e.g., embeds tracking pixels, sets third-party cookies, syncs IDs), Lens detects the discrepancy and offers remediation.

prerequisites.description: User's browser must be configured to send GPC=1 (Firefox, Brave, DuckDuckGo, Privacy Badger all send by default).
User is a resident of California, Colorado, Connecticut, Oregon, or another state recognizing GPC.

template.complaintSubject: Global Privacy Control signal not honored
template.complaintBodyTemplate: On {date}, I visited {url} while my browser was sending a Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal. The site {observedBehavior}. Under {jurisdiction} law ({regulationCitation}), businesses must treat GPC as a valid opt-out of sale/sharing of personal data. I am filing this complaint to request {requestedRemedy}.

successSignals.description: Page no longer sets tracking/analytics cookies on subsequent visits from same browser session.
Facebook/Google/X pixels no longer fire on page load.
Complaint receipt confirmed by CA or CO AG office.
